nodes.name: Biology
nodes.description: Life sciences: cells, genetics, evolution, and taxonomy

items.prompt: Which 18th-century Swedish botanist created the binomial nomenclature system for naming species, still used in modern taxonomy?
items.explanation:
Born 1707 in Råshult, Sweden; died 1778 in Uppsala
Published *Systema Naturae* (1735); 10th edition (1758) is the formal starting point of modern zoological nomenclature
Binomial nomenclature: every species named with two Latin words — genus + specific epithet (e.g., *Homo sapiens*)
Formalized the hierarchy of taxonomic ranks: kingdom, class, order, genus, species (modern system adds phylum, family, domain)
Named himself *Homo sapiens* ('wise man') in the 1758 edition; considered the 'father of modern taxonomy'

items.prompt: What is the binomial (scientific) name for the modern human species, coined by Linnaeus in 1758?
items.explanation:
Latin for 'wise man'; genus *Homo* + species *sapiens*
Named by Linnaeus in the 10th edition of *Systema Naturae* (1758)
Modern humans (*H. s. sapiens*) are sometimes distinguished as a subspecies from archaic *H. s. idaltu*
Full taxonomy: Domain Eukarya → Kingdom Animalia → Phylum Chordata → Class Mammalia → Order Primates → Family Hominidae → Genus *Homo* → Species *sapiens*
Oldest confirmed *Homo sapiens* fossils: ~300,000 years ago from Jebel Irhoud, Morocco

items.prompt: What biological process, occurring primarily in chloroplasts, converts sunlight, CO₂, and water into glucose and oxygen, forming the base of nearly all food chains?
items.explanation:
Overall equation: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂
Two stages: light-dependent reactions (in thylakoid membranes) and the Calvin cycle (in the stroma)
Chlorophyll absorbs mostly red and blue light and reflects green — hence plants appear green
Not exclusive to plants: algae and cyanobacteria also photosynthesize
Ancient cyanobacteria caused the 'Great Oxidation Event' ~2.4 billion years ago, filling Earth's atmosphere with oxygen

items.prompt: What organelle, found in nearly all eukaryotic cells, produces most of the cell's ATP through cellular respiration and contains its own DNA?
items.explanation:
Called the 'powerhouse of the cell'; generates ATP via oxidative phosphorylation in the inner membrane
Have their own circular DNA (mtDNA), supporting the endosymbiotic theory: they were once free-living alpha-proteobacteria
Mitochondrial DNA is inherited only from the mother; used to trace maternal ancestry ('Mitochondrial Eve')
A single cell can contain hundreds to thousands of mitochondria depending on its energy demands
Mitochondrial dysfunction underlies many diseases including some forms of diabetes, deafness, and neurodegeneration

items.prompt: What type of cell division produces two genetically identical diploid daughter cells from a single parent cell, used for growth, repair, and asexual reproduction?
items.explanation:
Produces two diploid cells identical to the parent cell
Phases: Prophase → Metaphase → Anaphase → Telophase → Cytokinesis (mnemonic: PMAT-C)
Used for somatic (body) cell division: growth, wound healing, and cell replacement
Contrasted with meiosis: reduces chromosome number by half to produce haploid gametes for sexual reproduction
Cancer is characterized by uncontrolled mitosis; oncogenes accelerate it and tumor suppressor genes (e.g., p53) normally prevent it
items.cardFront: Cell division → 2 genetically identical diploid cells

items.prompt: What type of cell division, occurring in the gonads, reduces the chromosome number by half and produces four genetically distinct haploid cells for sexual reproduction?
items.explanation:
Produces four haploid cells (sperm or eggs), each with half the parent cell's chromosome number
Two successive divisions: Meiosis I (homologous pairs separate) and Meiosis II (sister chromatids separate)
Crossing over (recombination) during Meiosis I shuffles genetic material between chromosomes, generating diversity
Errors in meiosis can produce gametes with incorrect chromosome numbers (aneuploidies); in fertilization these cause conditions like Down syndrome
Contrasted with mitosis, which produces two identical diploid somatic cells
items.cardFront: Cell division → 4 genetically distinct haploid gametes

items.prompt: What fundamental principle of molecular biology, named by Francis Crick in 1958, describes the flow of genetic information from DNA to RNA to protein?
items.explanation:
Coined by Francis Crick in 1958; formally published in *Nature* in 1970
Transcription: DNA → mRNA (in the nucleus, using DNA as a template)
Translation: mRNA → protein (at the ribosome in the cytoplasm, using mRNA as a template)
'Central Dogma' is often overstated as absolute; reverse transcription (RNA → DNA) occurs in retroviruses like HIV
CRISPR gene editing tools modify the DNA step, permanently altering the genetic message for all descendant cells

items.prompt: Which American microbiologist proposed the three-domain classification of life in 1977 — Bacteria, Archaea, and Eukarya — based on ribosomal RNA sequences?
items.explanation:
Born 1928 in Syracuse, NY; died 2012 in Urbana, Illinois
Used 16S ribosomal RNA sequences to compare organisms across life; discovered Archaea are as distinct from bacteria as bacteria are from eukaryotes
Revolutionized the tree of life, replacing the earlier five-kingdom (Whittaker) and two-kingdom systems
His work faced years of resistance from the biological establishment before molecular evidence made it undeniable
Never won a Nobel Prize despite nominations; received the Crafoord Prize (2003) and the National Medal of Science

items.prompt: Which kingdom of life contains eukaryotic organisms with chitin cell walls that obtain nutrients by absorbing decomposed matter, and includes yeasts, molds, and mushrooms?
items.explanation:
Eukaryotic, heterotrophic (cannot photosynthesize); obtain nutrients by secreting digestive enzymes and absorbing the breakdown products
Cell walls contain chitin (the same polymer as insect exoskeletons), unlike plant cell walls (cellulose)
More closely related to animals than to plants, phylogenetically
Ecological role: primary decomposers, recycling carbon and nutrients back into ecosystems
Humongous Fungus in Oregon (*Armillaria ostoyae*) is possibly the largest single organism on Earth by area (~9.6 km² of mycelium, estimated 8,000 years old)
items.cardFront: Chitin cell walls; yeasts, molds, mushrooms

items.prompt: What term describes the complete set of genetic material (DNA) within an organism, including all of its genes and non-coding sequences?
items.explanation:
The human genome contains ~3.2 billion base pairs organized into ~20,000–25,000 protein-coding genes
Only ~1.5% of the human genome codes for proteins; the rest includes regulatory sequences, introns, and non-coding RNA
The Human Genome Project (1990–2003) produced the first draft; a complete, error-free sequence was published in 2022
*Escherichia coli* has ~4.6 million base pairs; the human genome is ~700× larger
The largest known genome belongs to *Paris japonica* (a Japanese flower) at ~150 billion base pairs — ~47× the human genome
items.cardFront: Complete genetic material of an organism

items.prompt: What single-stranded RNA molecule, synthesized from a DNA template during transcription, carries the genetic code from the nucleus to the ribosome where proteins are assembled?
items.explanation:
mRNA = messenger RNA; carries codon sequences (triplets that specify amino acids) from DNA to ribosomes
The genetic code is nearly universal: the same 64 codons specify the same amino acids in nearly all organisms
After translation, mRNA is degraded; the ephemeral nature of mRNA allows rapid changes in gene expression
mRNA vaccines (BioNTech/Pfizer, Moderna COVID-19 vaccines) introduce synthetic mRNA that instructs cells to produce a viral antigen
Katalin Karikó and Drew Weissman received the 2023 Nobel Prize for modifications that stabilize mRNA for therapeutic use
items.cardFront: Carries genetic code from nucleus to ribosome

items.prompt: What mechanism of evolutionary change, proposed by Charles Darwin and Alfred Russel Wallace in 1858, states that organisms better adapted to their environment tend to survive and reproduce more successfully?
items.explanation:
Proposed simultaneously by Darwin and Wallace; jointly presented to the Linnean Society of London on July 1, 1858
Four requirements: variation in traits, heritability of traits, differential survival, and differential reproduction
Operates on phenotype but selects for genotype; genetic mutations are the ultimate source of heritable variation
*On the Origin of Species* (Darwin, 1859) laid out the full evidence and argument
Artificial selection (selective breeding) is the human-directed analog; Darwin used it as his opening analogy in *Origin*
items.cardFront: Mechanism of evolution; survival of the fittest
